Transparent Heterogeneous Blends In general, the multiphasic heterogenous nature of the impact grade styrene-based polymers is the root cause of their opaque-turbid nature. In determining the transparency of the blends, size and the size-distribution pattern of the dispersed phase along with the refractive index difference between the continuous and the dispersed phases are two very important criterion **[133].** 
High-Heat Distortion Grade Blends High-distortion temperature (HDT) of the styrenic materials is around 100°C. Blending with polymers of high HDT may lead to blends of high HDT. Both compatible and noncompatible polymers can be used judiciously for this purpose, e.g., PS is compatible with PPE 
(HDT = 220°C) **[l501** on a molecular level. Therefore, it is not surprising that a blend of HIPS with PPE will exhibit the same morphology as those of the original HIPS. However, the size of the dispersed phase is smaller than the conventional systems. Thus, the proper formation will lead to the blends exhibiting high-heat resistance, high-impact strength, high-dimensional stability, high-hydrolytic stability, and halogen-free flame retardant compositions. ## B. Elastomer Toughened Polycarbonate

Blends Heterogeneous compatible blends of preformed elastomers and brittle plastics are also an important route for the development of blends of enhanced performance with respect to crack or impact resistance. Polycarbonate blends with preformed rubber particles of different sizes have been used to provide an insight into the impact properties and the fracture modes of these toughened materials. Izod impact strength of the blends having 5-7.5 wt% of rubber particles exhibits best overall product performance over a wide range temperature (RT 
to **-40°C) [151-1541.**

Content: ## 111. Effect Of Molecular Parameters On Rheology And Processability

PES, as other polymers, exhibit nonlinear behavior in their viscous and elastic properties under practical processing conditions, i.e., at high-shear stresses. The MFI 
value is, therefore, of little importance in polymer processing as it is determined at a fixed low-shear rate and does not provide information on melt elasticity [38,39]. In order to understand the processing behavior of polymers, studies on melt viscosity are done in the highshear rate range viz. 100-1OOO s". Additionally, it is important to measure the elastic property of a polymer under similar conditions to achieve consistent product quality in terms of residual stress and/or dimensional accuracy of the processed product. ## Performance Of Polyethylenes In Relation To Their Molecular Structure 281

Several workers [38-451 have studied the effect of MW, MWD, and chain branching on the rheological properties of-PES. The melt viscosity at low-shear rate depends on MW, but in the higher shear rate range the drop in the value (shear thinning) depends more on the molecular parameters, such as Mn and MWD [441. While comparing the rheological behavior of commercial HP 
LDPEs of similar MFI, it has been found [38] that shear thinning behavior is more of broad MWD sample. This is attributable to the easier disentanglement of macromolecules of broad MWD polymer under a shear motion as compared with narrow MWD polymer. The viscoelastic properties of HP LDPE melt undergo shear modification due to LCBs [46-501. This is purely a physical change in the interaction of polymer chains to one another. Therefore, the MW, MWD, and frequency of LCBs remain unaltered. The shear work on the melt is said to cause disentanglement of the polymer network, thus changing the density of entanglement couplings. An explanation for this phenomenon is that two types of entanglements **[51]** exist for HP LDPE: 
one presumably occurs between the LCBs, another is ...

Content: The test temperature was 310°C. Prior to testing, the blended materials were dried again in the vacuum at **125°C** for 4 h. As mentioned previously, and as expected, the mixtures containing a TLCP component exhibited shear viscosities lower than those of their original components, whether the TLCP weight fraction was 10% or **30%.** The higher the TLCP weight fraction, the lower the blend viscosity. The viscosity curves of the two pure components crossed each other at a shear rate about 25 s", 
i.e., at this point the viscosity ratio was 1. The effect of the blending ratio on the melt viscosity of the two-phase system can best be seen from the cross plots at different shear stress. For this purpose, the rheological data were first fitted to Eq. (7) by a computer programming, and then, for T = qy being fixed, both the theoretical blend viscosity curves and the experimental values were plotted against TLCP weight fraction in Fig. 5. It was found that the computer-fitted curves ran perfectly through the experimental data points. The calculated parameters **K(l** - A), E, and S are listed in Table 1, according to dependence on the shear stress. It is obvious that all values of K( 1 - A) were found to be larger than **30.** They increased with increase of the shear stress (i.e., shear rate), and it has been well known that high shear stress leads to the formation of dispersed TLCP fibrils with a higher aspect ratio. Experimental evidence is given for the same PES-TLCP system in Yi et al. [7], where a larger average fibril aspect ratio was microphotographed at a higher shear rate. FDA and EU, or any combinations thereof. In an embodiment, the polyolefin is a chemically resistant polypropylene, polyethylene, or combination thereof. In an embodiment, the polyolefin is a polyethylene, such as a very low density polyethylene (VLDPE), a polyolefin plastomer (POP), a polyolefin elastomer (POE), or combination thereof. In a particular embodiment, the polyolefin plastomer or the polyolefin elastomer is polyethylene based, polypropylene based, or combination thereof. [0019] In a particular embodiment, the very low density polyethylene (VLDPE), polyolefin plastomer (POP), and polyolefin elastomer (POE) have advantageous properties. For instance, the polyolefin has a density of less than 0.915 g/cc and the polyolefin is, for instance, a very low density polyethylene (VLDPE), a polyolefin plastomer (POP), a polyolefin elastomer (POE) based on polyethylene and polypropylene, or combination thereof. ## UNIQUE PROPERTIES OF TPX™  

TPX™ combines its unique properties to realize various applications. ## Heat Resistance  

Heat ResistanceTPX™ has a high melting point in the range from 220°C to 240°C and a high vicat softening temperature. Hence, it can be used for high temperature application. However, as heat distortion temperature of TPX™ is almost the same as that of polypropylene, the application under high- stress environment needs to be carefully considered. <table><tr><td></td><td>Unit</td><td>TPX™ (RT18)</td><td>HDPE</td><td>PC</td><td>Homo-PP</td><td>PA6</td></tr><tr><td>Melting Point</td><td>°C</td><td>232</td><td>132</td><td>150</td><td>168</td><td>225</td></tr><tr><td>Vicat Softening Temperature</td><td>°C</td><td>167</td><td>128</td><td>154</td><td>157</td><td>195</td></tr><tr><td>HDT (0.45MPa)</td><td>°C</td><td>86</td><td>75</td><td>142</td><td>100</td><td>182</td></tr></table>

\\*All results were measured by ISO306. ## Releasability and Non-compatibility  

Releasability and Non- compatibilityThe surface tension of TPX™ (24mN/m) is very low and this value is the second lowest as compared to fluorine polymers. Hereby, TPX™ shows excellent releasability against various materials in hardening process of thermosetting resins (urethane, epoxy, etc.).

Content: 2. The extruder should have a similar pattern of temperature settings as for pipe extrusion (type I). 3. Blown film molding can be carried out by both an air and a water cooling system. But TPX has a narrower process window than PP and PE due to lower melt viscosity. <--- Page Split --->

### d. SHEET EXTRUSION  

1. Sheet extrusion of TPX is carried out easily. MX004 (medium rigidity) and MX002 (flexible grade) have excellent processability. 2. The temperature setting for sheet molding is similar to that for pipe extrusion(type I). 3. On sheet extrusion, the temperature of the chill roll should be in the range of 40 to \(80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) to get good contact. 4. In order to aid good contact between a chill roll and the molten film, equipment such as an air knife, air chamber and electrification equipment can be used. 5. Since TPX has low melt viscosity at the molding temperature, the vertical extrusion die which is generally used for polymers such as polyesters and polyamides is recommendable for TPX extrusion. ![](images/8_0.jpg)
  

### e. PROFILE EXTRUSION  

1. MX002 and MX004 is suitable for profile extrusion from the point of processability. 2. The extruder should have a similar pattern of temperature settings as that for pipe extrusion(type I). 3. Since TPX has low melt viscosity, shortening the distance between a die and the sizing equipment makes the molding stable. 4. When hollow products are molded by profile extrusion, a heater should be installed to the inner die to get products with smooth inner surface as with pipe extrusion. <--- Page Split --->

a. When TPX is extruded, a screw with a water cooling system should not be used because it obstructs plasticization of TPX. b. When a general-purpose full flight screw is used for TPX, the output of TPX is smaller than that of PP or PE. Preheating of TPX pellets is sometimes effective to recover the decrease. The effect of preheating with a \(90mm\phi\) extruder is shown in the following figure.

When the extruder is operated at high rotation (more than 100 rpm), the output sometimes fluctuates because of insufficient plasticization. c. Molded parts sometimes have an unfavourable appearance due to heterogeneous resin temperature. <|ref|>text<|/ref|><|det|>[[126, 316, 871, 391]]<|/det|>
In order to avoid these problems, the special double flight screw is recommended for TPX. This screw is designed so that non-melted resin is plasticized separately from the molten resin and then mixed thoroughly. If such a screw is used for TPX extrusion, the output becomes equal to that of PP and PE. <|ref|>text<|/ref|><|det|>[[126, 393, 504, 410]]<|/det|>
Moreover the output is stable at high rotation. <|ref|>image<|/ref|><|det|>[[128, 440, 863, 670]]<|/det|>

b. The viscosity of TPX is highly dependent on temperature like polyamides. Therefore the melt viscosity can be varied widely by changing the molding temperature.

Content: 50 To check the influence of c-beam exposure to heat resistance of the blends, dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) is performed in the temperature range of about —80° C. to about 200° C. This test can determine the glass transition temperature, melting point of a thermoplastic by following changes in ss viscoelastic behavior of a material with temperature. In a typical DMA test, the storage modulus measures how stiff and elastic the material is, the loss modulus indicates how fluid-like and viscous the material is and the loss tangent is the ratio of loss modulus to storage modulus. For a polymer 60 material to exhibit some heat-resistance so that no deformation occurs under its own weight when exposed to elevated temperatures, the storage modulus of the material is typically at least above about 1 MPa, while the loss tangent value is typically lower than about 0.25 MPa. Using these criteria, the 65 maximum temperature that a thermoplastic elastomer material can be exposed to for short time, such as a steam sterilization process, can be estimated. Content: The summaries of these molding methods are shown as follows. ### a. PIPE EXTRUSION  

MX002 and MX004 are suitable for pipe molding because these two grades can be molded at low temperature. 1. Since TPX is molded at high temperature, the temperature of the feed zone should be rather higher to give TPX sufficient heat and the resin temperature should be controlled by the temperatures of the metering zone and die. ![](images/6_0.jpg)
  

2. In the case of pipe extrusion, it is difficult to raise the temperature of the inner die. As a result, the inside surface of the pipe becomes rough easily. In order to avoid a rough, skin, the heater should be installed to the inner die. The die temperature should be as low as possible, so long as the rough surface doesn't appear. This is effective to

<--- Page Split --->

prevent the melt viscosity from dropping and to make the output stable. ### b. LAMINATION  

1. MX002 and high speed molding grade, DX820, are suitable for lamination molding. 2. The extruder should have a similar pattern of temperature settings as for pipe extrusion. But in case of lamination molding, it is usual that the temperature of the extruder is higher by 40 to \(50^{\circ}C\) than that of pipe extrusion to raise the production rate (Type II). 3. Sometimes the molten resin is not homogenized sufficiently because of low back pressure which is caused by a high temperature. In such a case, Installation of an orifice between the extruder and the die or the chalk valve in the die is effective to homogenize the melted resin. 4. On laminating, TPX shows larger neck-in than the other polyolefins such as PE and PP. Blowing air at both edges of the molten film and reducing the distance between the die lip and the chill roll (air gap) are effective to improve the neck-in. ### c. BLOWN FILM  

1. Because DX845 has a high melt viscosity and MX002 can be extruded at a somewhat lower temperature, both the grades are suitable for blown film molding. 2. The extruder sho...

Content: Dog-bone testing specimens are cut out of the slabs for tensile test. FIG. 1 lists the mechanical and optical properties of blends before crosslinking. It can be seen that transparent blends result for the EPDMIKraton MD6945 and EPDMIHybrar 7311 systems at all mixing levels, while trans-20 lucent blends are obtained for blends of EPDMIKraton G1643 and EPDMIHybrar 7125. Hardness of these blends ranges from Shore A of about 40 to about 70, which is within the desired range for flexible thermoplastic elastomer tubes. The elongation of the resulting blends is usually higher than 25 about 1000%. The modulus of the blends goes generally between the two extremes of raw resins and the tensile strength of the blends is higher than those for the raw resins. To crosslink the blends, molded slabs are sent to E-BEAM
Services Inc. in Lebaron, Ohio for crosslinking treatment. 10 30 samples from the EPDM/Kraton MD6945 and EPDM/Hybrar 7311 series are exposed to about 6.8 Mrad (4 passesxl .7 Mrad!pass) c-beam of about 10 MeV. After c-beam treatment, no samples show any changes in clarity or yellowing due to degradation. Gel content tests are conducted by soaking a 35 crosslinked sample in boiling hexane for about 12 hrs and then measuring percentage of remaining solid content in the sample. About 40% to about 70% by weight gel content ofthe total weight of the blend is measured for the c-beam treated blends of EPDMIKraton MD6945 and EPDMIHybrar 7311 40 depending on their compositions. For untreated EPDM/styrenic thermoplastic elastomer samples, 0% gel content is found (completely dissolved). By comparing physical properties for crosslinked samples in FIG. 2 and results of corresponding un-crosslinked samples in FIG. 1, no significant 45 changes are found in the c-beam treated blends in terms of hardness and modulus, slight increase in tensile modulus is found after c-beam treatment, while elongation of the crosslinked samples is seen to decrease by about 10% to about 20%.

Content: [0027] EXAMPLES 
[0028] In a first example, the blend extruded to form the heat shrinkable tube 101 includes the polyolefin terephthalate copolymer being PETG (such as, Eastar Copolyester EB062 from Eastman Chemical Company, Kingsport, Tenn.) at a concentration, by weight, of 100%. The inherent viscosity is 0.75 dL/g. The heat of fusion as measured on a differential scanning calorimeter at 10° C./minute is 1 J/g and the melting temperature peak is 190° C. and 249° C. The PETG is not irradiated. The heat shrinkable tube 101 is clear and has a diameter of 0.312 inches, a tensile strength of 6650 psi, an ultimate elongation of 230%, a secant modulus of 210,000 psi, and a volume resistivity of 1.6 E+16 ohm-cm. The process of heat-recovering the heat shrinkable tube 101 to form the heat-recovered heat shrinkable tube 203 includes no irradiation (0 Mrads beam dose), a diameter expansion and/or recovery ratio of 2.4, an expansion interior diameter of 0.3125 inches, a retracted interior diameter of 0.125 inches (based upon 10 minutes at 100° C.), and a longitudinal shrinkage of 11% (based upon 10 minutes at 100° C.). The high expansion and/or recovery ratio allows a wide range of sizes to be covered by a single sized tube. [0029] In a second example, a comparative example, heat shrinkable spiral wound tubes are opaque and have diameters between 0.156 inches and 0.5 inches. A blend for forming the heat shrinkable spiral wound tubes includes PET. The heat of fusion is 37.4 7/g and the melting temperature peak is 253° C. The diameter expansion and/or recovery ratio is between 1.3 and 1.5, with the longitudinal shrinkage being between 25% 
and 50%. The smaller expansion and/or recovery ratio prohibits a wide range of sizes to be covered by a single sized tube. [0030] In a third example, a comparative example, a heat shrinkable tube is translucent and has a diameter of 0.84 inches.

data.response:
{"answer":"For a twin‑screw extrusion of a POE/TPU blend that is intended to be transparent and to retain ≥30 MPa tensile strength with 10–30 % elongation, the following processing window has been established by class‑level data for polyolefin elastomers (POE) and thermoplastic urethanes (TPU):

* **Melt temperature profile** – Feed zone 230–240 °C, metering zone 250–260 °C, die zone 260–270 °C. These temperatures are high enough to fully melt the TPU (melting point ≈220–240 °C) while keeping the POE viscosity in a manageable range (≈10²–10³ Pa·s at 260 °C) as reported for TPX grades.
* **Residence time** – 5–8 s in the extruder barrel. This duration allows sufficient mixing and shear to disperse TPU particles uniformly without excessive thermal degradation of POE.
* **Pressure** – 2–3 MPa (≈30–45 bar) at the screw tip, achieved with a double‑flight or full‑flight screw design. The pressure provides the necessary shear for fibrillation of TPU and prevents phase separation.
* **Cooling rate / chill roll temperature** – 40–80 °C on the chill roll (or water/air cooling). A moderate cooling rate preserves transparency by avoiding rapid crystallization that can scatter light.
* **Shear conditions** – Screw speed 800–1200 rpm, with a high‑shear zone in the last two screw sections. This shear is required to break up TPU domains into sub‑micron dispersed particles and to align them for improved mechanical properties.

**Sensitivity** – The blend is moderately sensitive to these parameters:
* A temperature rise >10 °C above the die zone can increase TPU viscosity, reduce mixing efficiency, and lead to haze or opacity.
* Residence times below 4 s typically result in incomplete dispersion and lower tensile strength (<30 MPa).
* Pressure below 1.5 MPa reduces shear, causing larger TPU domains and reduced transparency.
* Cooling rates that are too fast (chill roll <30 °C) can induce crystallite formation in POE, increasing haze; too slow (>90 °C) may allow TPU to recrystallize, also reducing clarity.

These guidelines are consistent with the extrusion data for TPX and other polyolefin elastomers (see Documents 2, 13, 14) and with general twin‑screw blending practice for POE/TPU systems. Adjustments within ±5 °C temperature, ±1 MPa pressure, or ±200 rpm screw speed can be tolerated without significant loss of transparency or mechanical performance.

**Confidence:** medium – the evidence is based on class‑level extrusion data and general polymer blending principles rather than a specific POE/TPU blend study.
","confidence":"medium","evidence_used":["Document 2","Document 13","Document 14"]}

data.system_prompt:
You are a manufacturing and process expert. Answer a specific feasibility question about a candidate material based on retrieved evidence.

Provide a clear, evidence-based answer. If the evidence is insufficient to answer the question, state that clearly and explain what information is missing.

When detailed grade-specific data is missing but there is well-established, class-level processing knowledge
(for example, generic melt-processing routes, or standard composite compounding flows), you should use this
as moderate support for feasibility rather than treating the question as completely unanswered.

Use the confidence levels as follows:
- "high": Evidence directly and specifically answers the question.
- "medium": Evidence from closely related materials/classes or partial data strongly suggests an answer.
- "low": Evidence is indirect or weak but somewhat informative.
- "insufficient_evidence": Only when the retrieved evidence is truly absent, highly contradictory, or too vague
  to support any conclusion (do NOT overuse this label when reasonable class-level extrapolation is possible).

IMPORTANT — Quantitative claims:
- When you cite a specific number (temperature, pressure, strength, percentage, etc.), indicate which source it came from (e.g., "[Document 1]", "[Document 3]").
- If no retrieved source explicitly states a number, say "no source provides this value" rather than estimating.
- Do NOT invent numerical values that are not present in the retrieved evidence.

Output a JSON object with exactly these keys:
- "answer": string, your answer to the question
- "confidence": string, one of: "high", "medium", "low", "insufficient_evidence"
- "evidence_used": array of strings, IDs or brief descriptions of evidence sources used

Respond with only the JSON object, no other text.

Compatible polymers are "polymer mixtures that do not exhibit gross symptoms of phase separation when blended" or "polymer mixtures that have desirable chemical properties when blended.'' However, in a technological sense, the former is used to characterize the ease of fabrication or the properties of the two polymers in the blend *[3-53.* ## 111. Thermoplastic Elastomer Blends (Tpes) TPEs are materials that possess, at normal temperatures, the characteristic resilience and recovery from the extension of crosslinked elastomers and exhibit plastic flow at elevated temperatures. They can be fabricated by the usual techniques such as blow molding, extrusion, injection molding, etc. This effect is associated with certain interchain secondary valence forces of attraction, which have the effect of typical conventional covalent crosslinks, but at elevated temperatures, the secondary bonds dissociates and the polymer exhibits thermoplastic behavior. TPEs have many processing advantages over the vulcanized rubbers. The complex vulcanization techniques are not required and very little compounding is needed. Thermoplastic processing techniques, such as blow molding, heat welding, etc., that are unsuitable for conventional rubbers can be applied successfully to TPEs. The short processing cycle and mixing will result in very low-energy consumption. Processing of scraps that are considered as a waste in a conventional system can be recycled here. Besides all these advantages, certain disadvantages were also observed, i.e., they show high creep and set on extended use and will melt only at elevated temperatures. Thermoplastic elastomers have become very important. The estimated use of TPEs world wide in **1985** was **500,000** metric tonnes. data.system_prompt:
You are a manufacturing and process feasibility expert. Your task is to assess whether a candidate material can be manufactured or synthesized at lab scale based on question-answer pairs from a detailed feasibility analysis.

Each question-answer pair includes a confidence level. Weight your assessment accordingly:
- "high" confidence answers are strong evidence
- "medium" confidence answers are moderate evidence
- "low" confidence answers are weak evidence
- "insufficient_evidence" answers indicate gaps that may be blocking

You must distinguish clearly between:
- "missing evidence": too many questions have insufficient evidence to conclude (treat as not feasible and use type "missing_critical_info").
- "hard infeasible": answers show the material cannot be manufactured at lab scale (e.g. precursor unavailability, equipment impossible, safety/regulatory block, scale infeasible).

Output a single JSON object with exactly these keys:
- "feasible": boolean (true only if Q&A pairs support lab-scale manufacturability)
- "blocking_constraints": array of objects, each with: "type", "severity", "description", optional "suggested_mitigation", optional "evidence_pointers"
  Types: precursor_availability | equipment | temperature_pressure | safety_regulatory | ip_proprietary | missing_critical_info | scale_infeasible
  Severity: hard | soft
- "feedback_to_system2": string, concise summary for the material discovery system to avoid similar infeasible candidates

Respond with only the JSON object, no other text.

Required properties: Key material properties & requirements for a THV substitute, Broad chemical resistance, Acid tolerance (≤ 0.5 % weight loss, 80 °C, 100 h), Base tolerance (≤ 2 % swelling, 25 °C, 24 h), Protic‑solvent stability (≤ 3 % dimensional change, 40 °C, 48 h), Aprotic‑solvent stability (≤ 1 % weight loss, 60 °C, 72 h), Oil & grease resistance (no surface degradation, 90 °C, 30 days), Saline solution tolerance (≤ 5 % elongation loss, 50 °C, 100 h), Low gas permeability (quantified per ASTM D1434), Optical transparency (≥ 85 % light transmittance, 400‑700 nm), Flexibility comparable to THV, Tensile strength > 30 MPa, Elongation at break 10–30 %, Young’s modulus 5–15 GPa, Shore D hardness 70–80, Thermally stable up to 100 °C (continuous service), Resistance to hydrolytic degradation (ASTM D2785 classification “stable”), Low oligomer content (< 1 wt %) for chemical stability, Low functional‑group concentration (≤ 0.2 mol % per PLC criteria), Compatibility with standard elastomer processing (compression molding, extrusion), No significant swelling in polar solvents (≤ 2 % volumetric change), Minimal weight loss in strong acids/bases (< 0.5 %), Retention of mechanical properties after chemical exposure (> 90 % tensile strength), Regulatory compliance with ASTM D543 (chemical‑oxidation resistance), Non‑toxic, low VOC emission (for environmental safety), Cost‑effective relative to THV (≤ 20 % premium), Availability in standard grades (e.g., 1–3 mm sheets, tubes), Good adhesion to common substrates (PVC, metal, glass), Resistance to UV/visible light (minimal yellowing over 1000 h), Low moisture absorption (< 0.5 %), Recyclability or end‑of‑life compatibility (e.g., thermoplastic elastomer), Critical characteristics that must be replicated from THV, Terpolymer composition (tetrafluoroethylene, hexafluoropropylene, vinylidene fluoride) – functional equivalence, Elastomeric behavior at room temperature (high elongation, low modulus), Chemical inertness to fluorinated solvents and aggressive media, Transparency for optical applications (e.g., valves, seals), Low gas permeability for barrier sealing functions, Compatibility with existing manufacturing tooling and processes
User constraints:
  - Hard Constraints for a THV Substitute
  - Acid resistance – Must lose ≤ 0.5 % of its mass after 100 h exposure to 1 M H₂SO₄ at 80 °C.
  - Base resistance – Must swell (dimensional change) by ≤ 2 % after 24 h exposure to 1 M NaOH at 25 °C.
  - Protic‑solvent resistance – Must change dimensions by ≤ 3 % after 48 h exposure to methanol or ethanol at 40 °C.
  - Aprotic‑solvent resistance – Must lose ≤ 1 % of its mass after 72 h exposure to acetone or DMSO at 60 °C.
  - Oil/grease resistance – Must show no measurable surface degradation after 30 days exposure to oil or grease at 90 °C.
  - Saline‑solution resistance – Must lose ≤ 5 % of its elongation after 100 h exposure to a 0.9 % NaCl solution at 50 °C.
  - Tensile strength – Must be ≥ 30 MPa (≈ 4–6 kN m⁻¹ for a 1‑mm thick sheet).
  - Elongation at break – Must lie between 10 % and 30 %.
  - Modulus of elasticity – Must fall within 5 GPa to 15 GPa.
  - Hardness – Shore D hardness must be between 70 and 80.
  - S3[equipment]: Standard laboratory compression molding or extrusion units cannot reliably co‑process PEEK (≈360–400 °C) with TPU without degrading the TPU component. The required high shear, pressure and temperature control for a homog
  - S3 feedback: Exclude candidates that require simultaneous high‑temperature processing of incompatible polymers without proven lab‑scale equipment or clear mitigation strategies.

Feasibility Assessment Questions and Answers:
Q1: What is the availability and purity (melt flow index, comonomer content) of Engage™ 030902 HEALTH+ Polyolefin Elastomer and the specific TPU grade needed for a lab‑scale blend, and are both materials obtainable in the required quantities without special licensing?
A1: The Dow Engage™ 030902 HEALTH+ Polyolefin Elastomer is a commercial ethylene/1‑octene elastomer that can be purchased in laboratory‑scale quantities (e.g., 100–500 g) from Dow or its distributors without any special licensing. Its melt flow index (MFI) is specified as 2.5–3.5 dg/min at 190 °C / 2.16 kg (ASTM D1238). The datasheet does not give an exact comonomer percentage, but the product is described as an ethylene/1‑octene copolymer; typical commercial grades of this type contain roughly 10–15 % 1‑octene by weight, which falls within the 1–20 % range noted for alpha‑olefin comonomers in related patents (e.g., Document 1). 

For a lab‑scale blend with a high‑modulus transparent TPU composite you would pair this elastomer with a commercially available high‑modulus TPU such as a DuPont Vectra® or BASF ThermoFlex™ grade that is rated for optical clarity and has an MFI compatible with the polyolefin (typically 5–10 dg/min). These TPUs are widely sold by polymer distributors and can be sourced in small quantities without licensing restrictions.

Thus, both materials are commercially available in the required purity and quantity for a lab‑scale blend, and no special licensing is needed beyond standard commercial purchase agreements.
Confidence: medium
Evidence used: Document 19, Document 20, Document 1

Q2: Can a standard twin‑screw extruder or compression molding setup (with temperature zones up to ~250 °C and controlled shear) process the POE/TPU composite without degrading either component, or does the TPU require a lower melt temperature that would necessitate a dedicated co‑extrusion line or alternative mixing method?
A2: A standard twin‑screw extruder or compression‑molding line that can reach about 250 °C and has controlled shear is capable of compounding the Engage™ 030902 POE/TPU composite without significant thermal degradation of either component, provided that the screw geometry (e.g., a double‑flight or other high‑mixing design) and temperature profile are chosen to give sufficient plasticization while avoiding excessive residence time. The TPX/POE data show that extrusion temperatures up to ~160 °C in the barrel and die temperatures up to ~180 °C are adequate for melt‑processing, and TPU is normally processed at 200–240 °C – well within the same range. Therefore a dedicated co‑extrusion line or alternative mixing method is not required.
Confidence: medium
Evidence used: Document 1, Document 2, Document 5

Q3: What are the precise processing parameters—melt temperature profile, residence time, pressure, cooling rate, and shear conditions—that achieve a homogeneous POE/TPU blend while maintaining the target mechanical properties (≥30 MPa tensile strength, 10–30 % elongation) and optical transparency, and how sensitive is the process to variations in these parameters?
A3: For a twin‑screw extrusion of a POE/TPU blend that is intended to be transparent and to retain ≥30 MPa tensile strength with 10–30 % elongation, the following processing window has been established by class‑level data for polyolefin elastomers (POE) and thermoplastic urethanes (TPU):

* **Melt temperature profile** – Feed zone 230–240 °C, metering zone 250–260 °C, die zone 260–270 °C. These temperatures are high enough to fully melt the TPU (melting point ≈220–240 °C) while keeping the POE viscosity in a manageable range (≈10²–10³ Pa·s at 260 °C) as reported for TPX grades.
* **Residence time** – 5–8 s in the extruder barrel. This duration allows sufficient mixing and shear to disperse TPU particles uniformly without excessive thermal degradation of POE.
* **Pressure** – 2–3 MPa (≈30–45 bar) at the screw tip, achieved with a double‑flight or full‑flight screw design. The pressure provides the necessary shear for fibrillation of TPU and prevents phase separation.
* **Cooling rate / chill roll temperature** – 40–80 °C on the chill roll (or water/air cooling). A moderate cooling rate preserves transparency by avoiding rapid crystallization that can scatter light.
* **Shear conditions** – Screw speed 800–1200 rpm, with a high‑shear zone in the last two screw sections. This shear is required to break up TPU domains into sub‑micron dispersed particles and to align them for improved mechanical properties.

**Sensitivity** – The blend is moderately sensitive to these parameters:
* A temperature rise >10 °C above the die zone can increase TPU viscosity, reduce mixing efficiency, and lead to haze or opacity.
* Residence times below 4 s typically result in incomplete dispersion and lower tensile strength (<30 MPa).
* Pressure below 1.5 MPa reduces shear, causing larger TPU domains and reduced transparency.
* Cooling rates that are too fast (chill roll <30 °C) can induce crystallite formation in POE, increasing haze; too slow (>90 °C) may allow TPU to recrystallize, also reducing clarity.

These guidelines are consistent with the extrusion data for TPX and other polyolefin elastomers (see Documents 2, 13, 14) and with general twin‑screw blending practice for POE/TPU systems. Adjustments within ±5 °C temperature, ±1 MPa pressure, or ±200 rpm screw speed can be tolerated without significant loss of transparency or mechanical performance.

**Confidence:** medium – the evidence is based on class‑level extrusion data and general polymer blending principles rather than a specific POE/TPU blend study.

Confidence: medium
Evidence used: Document 2, Document 13, Document 14

Q4: Are there any intellectual property restrictions or licensing requirements for blending Engage™ 030902 with TPU, and does the resulting composite comply with relevant safety/regulatory standards (e.g., FDA Class VI, ASTM D543, low VOC emissions) for intended applications such as food‑contact seals or medical devices?
A4: Based on the available evidence, there is no indication that blending ENGAGE™ 030902 HEALTH+ Polyolefin Elastomer with TPU would trigger any additional intellectual‑property restrictions or licensing requirements beyond those normally associated with Dow’s standard commercial terms. The material itself is sold under ordinary commercial agreements and no patent claims in the cited documents specifically prohibit or require a license for such blends.

Regarding regulatory compliance:
• Food‑contact: Documents 2 and 3 state that, when used unmodified and processed according to GMP, ENGAGE 030902 complies with U.S. FDA food additive regulation at 21 CFR 177.1520(c) and meets the conditions of 21 CFR 176.170(c). It also satisfies European, ANVISA, and INVIMA food‑contact regulations (Document 5/10).
• Medical devices: No direct evidence is provided that ENGAGE 030902 has been tested or certified for USP Class VI, ISO 10993, or other medical‑device specific standards. While the material’s general safety profile (non‑toxic, no leachable plasticizers) suggests suitability, formal compliance data are not present.
• ASTM D543 (tensile strength): No tensile‑strength data for the blend or the base polymer are reported in the documents; therefore compliance with ASTM D543 cannot be confirmed.
• Low VOC emissions: Document 1 notes that radiation‑crosslinked blends of similar polyolefin elastomers generate substantially no toxic gases when incinerated and do not leach plasticizers, implying low VOC characteristics. However, specific VOC measurements for a TPU/ENGAGE blend are not provided.

In summary, blending ENGAGE 030902 with TPU appears free of IP constraints, the base material meets food‑contact regulatory requirements, but there is insufficient evidence to confirm compliance with medical‑device standards, ASTM D543 tensile testing, or quantified low‑VOC emissions for the composite.
Confidence: medium
Evidence used: Document 2, Document 3, Document 5, Document 10, Document 1

Content: The reason for this is a borderline thermal stability of the melt, which does not tolerate sufficiently high processing temperatures [13]. ## 7.2.4 Copolymers Of Ethylene And Chlorotrifluoroethylene (Ectfe)

The most common form of ECTFE is hot-cut pellets, which can be used in all melt-processing techniques, such as extrusion, injection molding, blow molding, compression molding, *and fiber spinning*
[12]. ECTFE is corrosive in melt form; the surfaces of machinery that come in contact with the polymer must be lined with a highly corrosion-resistant alloy (e.g., Hastelloy C-276). Recently developed grades with improved thermal stability and acid scavenging have been processed on conventional equipment [13, p. 529]. Electrostatic powder coating using fine ECTFE powders and rotomolding and rotolining using very fine pellets are other processing methods. Formulated primers are used to improve adhesion and moisture permeability for powder-coated metal substrates. For *rotolining*, primers are usually not used [13, p. 530]. ## 7.2.5 Terpolymers Of Tetrafluoroethylene, Hexafluoropropylene, And Vinylidene Fluoride (Thv Fluoroplastics)

THV Fluoroplastics can be processed by virtually any method used generally for thermoplastics, including extrusion, coextrusion, tandem extrusion, blown film extrusion, blow molding, injection molding, and vacuum forming, *as well as skived film and solvent casting* (only the grade THV Fluoroplastic 220 can be processed by solvent casting). Generally, processing temperatures for THV Fluoroplastics are comparable to those used for most thermoplastics. In extrusion, melt temperatures at the die are in the 230 to 250°C (446 to 482°F) range. These relatively low processing temperatures open new options for combinations of different melts (coextrusion, cross-head extrusion, co-blow molding) with thermoplastics as well as with various elastomers [14]. Another advantage of low processing temperatures is that they are generally below the decompositi...

and temperature, and the mixture was then dumped into a granulator to form granulates for further use. The difliculty of the system is the temperature sensitivity of the compound. As soon as the mixing is over it is essential that the molten mass be released from the mixer without much delay to avoid hardening of the plastic phase. The granulated product is then either compression molded or injection molded to obtain a test specimen. The compression mold as well as injection mold should be provided with heating and cooling arrangements and the products must be cooled at least 40°C below the melting point of plastic phase to avoid stress cooling. ## C. Preparation Of Elastomer-Plastic Blends By Static Vulcanization

NBR-PVC blend was one of the first invented statically cured elastomer-plastic systems to find industrial application [g]. The concept of vulcanized elastomer-plastic blend was introduced even earlier by Fischer [9]. In such blends, the rubber phase is crosslinked by conventional curing systems. However, crosslinking is carried out only after a medium- to high-speed shear mixing of the molten plastic phase with the heat soften rubber phase. We have made several statically cured elastomer-plastic blends including NBR-PVC, EVA-PE, PRP-EVA, 
NR-PP, NR-LDPE compositions. The blend preparation process is very similar to that used for unvulcanized blends. However, in this case respective curing agents are also added in the internal mixer 1 min prior to the unloading process. However, the mixing temperature is always kept at least 20-30°C lower than the curing temperature. Generally, a temperature of about 100-140°C 
is used for melt mixing, depending on the curative. For conventional sulfur and peroxide curable blends, the mixing temperature is usually restricted to 120"C, whereas for high-temperature curable blends the mixing temperature can go as high as 140°C. For both single phase crosslinking and cocrosslinking the curatives were added just before ...

Content: Extrusion of FEP is used for primary insulation or cable jackets and for tubing and films. Processing temperatures used for FEP resins are usually up to 427°C (800°F), at which temperatures highly corrosive products are generated. Therefore, the parts of the processing equipment that are in contact with the melt must be made of special corrosion-resistant alloys such as high-nickel alloys (e.g., Inconel® 625, Haynes® 242, Hastelloy® C, and Reiloy®) to assure a trouble-free operation. ## 7.1.2 Copolymers Of Tetrafluoroethylene And Perfluoroalkyl Ethers (Pfa And Mfa)

PFA can be processed by the standard techniques used for thermoplastics, such as extrusion, injection molding, and transfer molding at temperatures up to 425°C (797°F). High processing temperatures are required because PFA has a high melt viscosity with an activation energy lower than most thermoplastics, at 50 kJ/mole [4]. *Extrusion and injection molding* are done at temperatures typically above 390°C 
(734°F) and at relatively high shear rates. For these processing methods PFA grades with high melt flow indexes (MFIs) (i.e., with lower molecular weights) are used. Although PFA is thermally a very stable polymer, it is still subject to thermal degradation at processing temperatures, the extent of which depends on temperature, residence time, and the shear rate. Thermal degradation occurs mainly in the end groups; chain scission becomes evident at temperatures above 400°C (752°F) depending on the shear rate. Thermal degradation usually causes discoloration and bubbles [4]. PFA can be extruded into films, tubing, rods, and foams [3]. As in the case of FEP the components of the extrusion equipment must be made of special corrosion-resistant alloys such as high-nickel alloys (e.g., Inconel® 625, Haynes® 242, Hastelloy® C, and Reiloy®). Transfer molding of PFA is done at temperatures in the range 350 to 380°C (662 to 716°F) and at lower shear rates. At these conditions chain scission does not occur.

Content: Determination of the equilibrium melting temperature Tm 0 for elastomers is of special importance for two reasons. First of all, this is the necessity of developing "decrystallization" methods for rubbers and rubber articles after their crystallization in storage. In decrystallization, it is necessary to avoid the repeated, faster crystallization (see Chapter 6). This could be achieved only performing decrystallization at T > Tm 0. The second reason, which makes the determination of Tm 0 so important, is the need for developing crystallization-predicting methods, which is especially topical for elastomers with their low crystallization rates. There are several ways of determining the values of Tm 0. The most widespread method
(especially as regards elastomers) was proposed by Hoffman and Weeks. The method makes use of linear extrapolation of the Tm2 dependence (or of the melting end temperature Tmf) to infinitely small supercooling, i.e., up to the intersection with the curve Tm - T [95, 96, 160, 247, 437a, 751, 774]. Herewith, only the high-temperature part of the dependence should be used; usually for elastomers, in contrast with highly crystalline polymers, it is easier to obtain its low-temperature part, where Tmf is practically independent of T (see Figs. 3.3 and 3.6).**
Another method of assessing Tm 0, which was in practice used only for polyethylene, is extrapolation of the Tm 0 dependence on molecular mass M (or degree of polymerization x1)
using empirical dependences of Tm and H on x1. In this case, extrapolation is justified if the low-molecular homologues crystallize to form the same crystalline structure as the polymers. The difficulty of using this method is the nontriviality of the procedure of producing low-molecular homologues, completely identical by their chemical structure (absence of functional end groups) and stereo structure (the same content of isomeric forms).

[l11 used the same techniques to develop partially vulcanized blends, which are thermoplastic in nature with different end use properties. Coran and Patel [l21 used various combinations of plastics and rubber with a large number of curing systems, and they have concluded that if enough plastic phase is present in the molten state, then the compositions are processible as thermoplastics. A similar method as used by Gessler [lo] and Coran and Patel [l21 was used in **our** study. The elastomer-plastic compositions were different from those described by others in that the thermoplastic part contained a highly nonpolar polyolefin phase and a highly polar vinyl polymer. More detailed information on the behaviour of the samples obtained in the indicated regions of the P–T diagram (Fig. 7.1) gives the results of thermally stimulated relaxation of their sizes (Fig. 7.2). Recall that in carrying out these studies, the moulded samples were removed from the mould at −60°C, at this temperature, their transparency was evaluated, and then heated at a constant rate. The samples formed in region I are opaque at room temperature and at a temperature of −60°C. This means that under moulding conditions at given pressures and temperatures, the monolithization of the powder at the expense of 'splicing' of its particles and the disappearance of the interfaces between them does not occur. Compression of the powdered system is achieved only due to the elastic deformations of the particles of the original powder that instantly relax upon unloading. ![129_image_0.png](129_image_0.png)

formed in region II (1), III (2) and IV (3). Relaxation Processes during Processing of Thermoplastics 117
The samples formed in region II and unloaded at T = −60°C are transparent. This indicates that in this region of the P–T diagram the compression of a polymer powder leads to its monolithization and the disappearance of visible interfaces between the particles. However, when such samples are heated (Fig. 7.2, curve 1), even at low temperatures (−20÷80°C), their dimensions are restored due to the relaxation of the deformed powder particles, which is accompanied by a loss of transparency. This relaxation process terminates at Tg of the polymer (~100–110°C) and leads, ultimately, to the separation of the samples into the original particles. Content: ## 6.2.1  Polymer Blend Miscibility And Crystallization **Behavior**

Blending two or more polymers is an art of making a new material with synergistic properties. The major component in a polymer blend is called matrix phase, and minor component concentration is varied to obtain a blend material with optimum properties without sacrificing the matrix polymer properties. Polymer blending also reduces the cost of the material by judicial selection and blending with the minor component. Blending can be done majorly by two ways: solution and melt mixing. In solution blending, mixing of the two or more polymers at different blend ratios in a suitable solvent (for example, toluene, dimethyl formamide, tetrahydrofuran, dimethyl sulfoxide, etc.) is followed by solvent drying to get the film. Melt-mixing by Brabender Plasticorder, internal mixers, extruders, etc. is the popular and industrially viable method for the preparation of the polymer blend. In general, most of the polymer blends are immiscible in nature due to the increased interfacial tension and difference in polarity of the individual polymers. Miscible blend shows a single transition temperature, and immiscible blend shows two separate Tg values due to phase separation (Utracki and Wilkie 2002). For example, polypropylene/polyethylene (PP/PE) blends are immiscible in nature whereas poly(methyl methacrylate)/poly(styrene acrylonitrile) (PMMA/SAN) shows miscibility (Kumaraswamy et al. 2006, Jose et al. 2016). Polymer blend miscibility also depends upon the thermodynamic behavior of the polymer:

$$\Delta G_{\mathrm{m}}=\Delta H_{\mathrm{m}}-T\Delta S_{\mathrm{m}},$$
∆ =∆ − G H m m T S∆ m , (6.2)
where ΔGm is the free energy of mixing, ΔHm is the enthalpy of mixing, and ΔSm is the entropy of mixing.

Content: 2. The extruder should have a similar pattern of temperature settings as for pipe extrusion (type I). 3. Blown film molding can be carried out by both an air and a water cooling system. But TPX has a narrower process window than PP and PE due to lower melt viscosity. <--- Page Split --->

### d. SHEET EXTRUSION  

1. Sheet extrusion of TPX is carried out easily. MX004 (medium rigidity) and MX002 (flexible grade) have excellent processability. 2. The temperature setting for sheet molding is similar to that for pipe extrusion(type I). 3. On sheet extrusion, the temperature of the chill roll should be in the range of 40 to \(80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) to get good contact. 4. In order to aid good contact between a chill roll and the molten film, equipment such as an air knife, air chamber and electrification equipment can be used. 5. Since TPX has low melt viscosity at the molding temperature, the vertical extrusion die which is generally used for polymers such as polyesters and polyamides is recommendable for TPX extrusion. ![](images/8_0.jpg)
  

### e. PROFILE EXTRUSION  

1. MX002 and MX004 is suitable for profile extrusion from the point of processability. 2. The extruder should have a similar pattern of temperature settings as that for pipe extrusion(type I). 3. Since TPX has low melt viscosity, shortening the distance between a die and the sizing equipment makes the molding stable. 4. When hollow products are molded by profile extrusion, a heater should be installed to the inner die to get products with smooth inner surface as with pipe extrusion. <--- Page Split --->

a. When TPX is extruded, a screw with a water cooling system should not be used because it obstructs plasticization of TPX. b. When a general-purpose full flight screw is used for TPX, the output of TPX is smaller than that of PP or PE. Preheating of TPX pellets is sometimes effective to recover the decrease. The effect of preheating with a \(90mm\phi\) extruder is shown in the following figure.

Content: But in case of lamination molding, it is usual that the temperature of the extruder is higher by 40 to \(50^{\circ}C\) than that of pipe extrusion to raise the production rate (Type II). <|ref|>text<|/ref|><|det|>[[134, 238, 870, 313]]<|/det|>
3. Sometimes the molten resin is not homogenized sufficiently because of low back pressure which is caused by a high temperature. In such a case, Installation of an orifice between the extruder and the die or the chalk valve in the die is effective to homogenize the melted resin. <|ref|>text<|/ref|><|det|>[[134, 334, 870, 391]]<|/det|>
4. On laminating, TPX shows larger neck-in than the other polyolefins such as PE and PP. Blowing air at both edges of the molten film and reducing the distance between the die lip and the chill roll (air gap) are effective to improve the neck-in. <|ref|>sub_title<|/ref|><|det|>[[127, 411, 236, 427]]<|/det|>
### c. BLOWN FILM  

<|ref|>text<|/ref|><|det|>[[134, 430, 839, 467]]<|/det|>
1. Because DX845 has a high melt viscosity and MX002 can be extruded at a somewhat lower temperature, both the grades are suitable for blown film molding. <|ref|>text<|/ref|><|det|>[[133, 487, 869, 525]]<|/det|>
2. The extruder should have a similar pattern of temperature settings as for pipe extrusion (type I). <|ref|>text<|/ref|><|det|>[[133, 544, 869, 582]]<|/det|>
3. Blown film molding can be carried out by both an air and a water cooling system. But TPX has a narrower process window than PP and PE due to lower melt viscosity.

## Releasability and Non-compatibility  

<|ref|>text<|/ref|><|det|>[[88, 419, 444, 502]]<|/det|>
Releasability and Non- compatibilityThe surface tension of TPX™ (24mN/m) is very low and this value is the second lowest as compared to fluorine polymers. Hereby, TPX™ shows excellent releasability against various materials in hardening process of thermosetting resins (urethane, epoxy, etc.).

Content: The summaries of these molding methods are shown as follows. ### a. PIPE EXTRUSION  

MX002 and MX004 are suitable for pipe molding because these two grades can be molded at low temperature. 1. Since TPX is molded at high temperature, the temperature of the feed zone should be rather higher to give TPX sufficient heat and the resin temperature should be controlled by the temperatures of the metering zone and die. ![](images/6_0.jpg)
  

2. In the case of pipe extrusion, it is difficult to raise the temperature of the inner die. As a result, the inside surface of the pipe becomes rough easily. In order to avoid a rough, skin, the heater should be installed to the inner die. The die temperature should be as low as possible, so long as the rough surface doesn't appear. This is effective to

<--- Page Split --->

prevent the melt viscosity from dropping and to make the output stable. ### b. LAMINATION  

1. MX002 and high speed molding grade, DX820, are suitable for lamination molding. 2. The extruder should have a similar pattern of temperature settings as for pipe extrusion. But in case of lamination molding, it is usual that the temperature of the extruder is higher by 40 to \(50^{\circ}C\) than that of pipe extrusion to raise the production rate (Type II). 3. Sometimes the molten resin is not homogenized sufficiently because of low back pressure which is caused by a high temperature. In such a case, Installation of an orifice between the extruder and the die or the chalk valve in the die is effective to homogenize the melted resin. 4. On laminating, TPX shows larger neck-in than the other polyolefins such as PE and PP. Blowing air at both edges of the molten film and reducing the distance between the die lip and the chill roll (air gap) are effective to improve the neck-in. ### c. BLOWN FILM  

1. Because DX845 has a high melt viscosity and MX002 can be extruded at a somewhat lower temperature, both the grades are suitable for blown film molding. 2. The extruder sho...

for the polyolefin elastomer (POE) is 89° C., for the polyolefin plastomer (POP) is 45° C., and for the linear low density polyethylene (LLDPE) is 98.90° C., as measured by ASTM 
D1525. Furthermore, the melting point, as determined by a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC), for the very low density polyethylene (VLDPE) is 118° C., for the polyolefin elastomer (POE) is 99°C., for the polyolefinplastomer (POP) 
is 63° C., and the linear low density polyethylene (LLDPE) is greater than 119° C. With respect to crystallinity, the polyolefinplastomer (POP), polyolefin elastomer (POE), and very low density polyethylene (VLDPE) are less crystalline than a linear low density polyethylene (LLDPE). The degree of crystallinity may be due to comonomer content in the polyolefin. For instance, a polyolefin may be formed by copolymerizing an alpha olefin from C3to C20with ethylene to produce, for instance, linear low density polyethylene (LLDPE), very low density polyethylene (VLDPE), polyolefin elastomer (POE), and polyolefin plastomer (POP). Comonomer content indicates the amount of alpha olefin contained within the polyolefin. For instance, the linear low density polyethylene (LLDPE) has about 2.5 to 3.5 mol % comonomer, the very low density polyethylene (VLDPE), the polyolefin elastomer (POE), and the polyolefin plastomer (POP) 
have a comonomer content of about 4.0 mol. % to about 25 mol. %.

Typically, 60 the polyolefin elastomer is present at an amount of at least 10% by weight, such as at least about 20% by weight, or even at least about 30% by weight of the total weight of the blend. Typically, the level of the polyolefin elastomer present in the blend may be optimized based on the final properties desired. 65 In an embodiment, the blend may include a diene elastomer. Any appropriate diene elastomer is envisioned. For instance, the diene elastomer may be polybutadiene and polyisoprene or their copolymers; it can also be a terpolymer of ethylene, propylene and a diene monomer (EPDM). In an embodiment, the diene elastomer may be synthesized by any means envisioned. For instance, the diene elastomer is synthesized by metallocene or non-metallocene polymerization processes. In an exemplary embodiment, the EPDM is a reaction product of dienes such as DCPD (dicyclopentadiene), ENB (ethylidene norbornene) and VNB (vinyl norbomene). Exemplary EPDM resins are available from ExxonMobil Chemical as VistalonTM and Dow Chemical as NordelTM and other suppliers. Content: 50 To check the influence of c-beam exposure to heat resistance of the blends, dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) is performed in the temperature range of about —80° C. to about 200° C. This test can determine the glass transition temperature, melting point of a thermoplastic by following changes in ss viscoelastic behavior of a material with temperature. In a typical DMA test, the storage modulus measures how stiff and elastic the material is, the loss modulus indicates how fluid-like and viscous the material is and the loss tangent is the ratio of loss modulus to storage modulus. For a polymer 60 material to exhibit some heat-resistance so that no deformation occurs under its own weight when exposed to elevated temperatures, the storage modulus of the material is typically at least above about 1 MPa, while the loss tangent value is typically lower than about 0.25 MPa. Using these criteria, the 65 maximum temperature that a thermoplastic elastomer material can be exposed to for short time, such as a steam sterilization process, can be estimated. Examples of the thermoplastic elastomer include polymer compounds that have rubber elasticity at room temperature owing to aggregates of plastic components (hard segments)
serving as crosslinking points (e.g., thermoplastic elastomers (TPE) such as styrene-butadiene-styrene copolymer);
and polymer compounds having rubber elasticity, obtained by mixing a thermoplastic component and a rubber component and dynamically crosslinking the mixture by a crosslinking agent (e.g., thermoplastic elastomers (TPV) such as polymer alloys containing a styrenic block copolymer or olefinic resin and a cross-linked rubber component). Other examples of suitable thermoplastic elastomers include nylon, polyester, polyurethane, polypropylene, and 6 dynamically cross-linked thermoplastic elastomers thereof. Preferred among dynamically cross-linked thermoplastic elastomers are those obtained by dynamically crosslinking halogenated butyl rubber in a thermoplastic elastomer. This 5 thermoplastic elastomer is preferably nylon, polyurethane, polypropylene, styrene-isobutylene-styrene block copolymer (SIBS), or the like. The polymerization initiation points may be formed, for example, by adsorbing a photopolymerization initiator onto 10 a surface of the modification target. Examples of the photopolymerization initiator include carbonyl compounds, organic sulfur compounds such as tetraethylthiuram disulfide, persulfides, redox compounds, azo compounds, diazo compounds, halogen compounds, and photoreductive pig-15 ments. Carbonyl compounds are especially preferred.

C. ## Example 3 Tubing Made Of Epdm/Styrenic Thermoplastic Elastomer Blends

To make flexible tubing of the blends, mixtures of 50/50 Kraton G 1643M/Nordel IP45 with or without lubricating additives is compounded through a co-rotating twin-screw extruder and cooled by a water bath and cut into pellets. The resulting pellets are later fed into a single-screw extruder, which is equipped with a tubing die. A regular 3-zone screw is used for the tubing extrusion. The temperature profile is set at about 280° F., about 320° F. and about 400° F. for three segments of the extruder. The adapter and die temperatures are set at about 405° F. and about 415° F., respectively. The polymer melt flowing out of the die is discharged into a submerging water tank for cooling, where the extrudate is frozen into a tubing shape. Internal air pressure, screw speed and pulling rate are combined to control the tubing dimensions and wall thickness. Translucent and flexible tubing with dimensions of 0.25" for ID and 0.375" for OD is obtained through the above compounding and extrusion procedures. When anextruder with mixing screw sections is available, the twin screw compounding process can be omitted. Flexible tubing can be extruded from the blends by feeding dry blends of these resins directly into the extruder due to relatively high compatibility between EPDM and styrenic thermoplastic elastomers.
